title: The Year of My Birth
paragraphs: “Have you become such a great man that you should write the story of your life?”
It was not someone else who asked me this question. My own heart asked it of me—and would not let me go until it had finished. Yet the answer, too, was safely in its keeping.
Do life and history belong only to the great? Do the small not own them too?

Every human being has a life story. He may never think of setting it down in writing. He may have no time to write it. Or he may not possess the skill to gather its scattered threads and weave them into a narrative.
But listen to two prisoners speaking between themselves: they are telling each other the stories of their lives.

Listen to two elderly rickshaw pullers resting beneath the shade of a tree, leaning at ease against their vehicles and exchanging the memorable episodes of their lives: there, too, are autobiographies.
Pay close attention to the conversation of two mendicants lying sleepless upon the raised platform of a travellers’ rest house: what unfolds there is again the history of their lives.

A patient on his deathbed, aware that the end has come, watches the story of his life circle silently before his mind’s eye. Unable to write it, unable even to speak it, he lets it run like a motion picture within his heart. The unconscious smile that crosses his face at the threshold of death, the deep sigh, the tear—each is a different chapter of his history.

The life of every individual may not have caused a great transformation in society. Political revolution may have found no place in his personal story. Yet, without even knowing it, he may have played some part in the changes and revolutions that swept through his country.
He is a limb of society. Society—and the nation itself—is nothing but the union of countless individuals like him. Who can deny this truth?
Therefore, he has some share, however small, in the transformations of his land. He may never have considered that share important enough to enter as a note in his own history.

In a storm that devastates an entire village, a torn branch is hurled through the air. It falls upon the façade of a house and brings it crashing down. What performed the deed—the storm or the branch?
Whatever the answer, an event has entered the history of that branch.
It was only after reflecting upon all this that I found the courage to render justice to my heart. The verdict came in the form of a question:
“Do life and history belong only to the great? Do the small not own them too?”
After that verdict, my pen began its work without pause or hindrance.
My birthday was celebrated on the third of June. I did not fail to remember what the philosopher Rousseau had said of birth:
“My first misfortune was that I was born a human being!”
There is something curious in the fact that the great thinker who instructed all humanity in the preservation of its birthrights should have cursed and lamented his own birth.

Perhaps the disorder surrounding the house, the country, and the political and social conditions into which he was born had deeply disturbed him. Perhaps, many years later, the memory of it made him reproach himself and ask, “Why was I ever born?”
My birth did not take place amid such circumstances.

My family was not immersed in wealth when I was born. Yet my parents were not submerged up to their necks in the dark pit of poverty. My motherland was indeed chained in slavery, but it was not—like the France into which Rousseau was born—reeling without direction and rushing towards destruction at a demonic speed.

Voltaire rejected Rousseau’s belief that, just as man enters the world weeping, he must spend his entire life sobbing and wasting away in futility. Like Voltaire, I too differ from Rousseau in my view of birth.

There are many days in a human life whose memory makes one groan and weep. I am no exception. Nevertheless, the day I was born, the day of my marriage, the day I first met the deity of my affection—my beloved leader—and the day I first entered prison in the struggle to defend my language: all these are days that bring me joy. They are memories in which I rejoice.

I have no faith in astrologers who calculate good and evil from the star under which one was born. But the year in which I was born was itself one of those years that foretold, with remarkable precision, both the good and the evil that awaited the world.

The pages that follow may assess the good and harm that came to the country because I was born; they may weigh the worth of the service I have rendered to my motherland. But when, in later years, I read and recalled the course of national events and international politics in the year of my birth, I felt only gladness.
The year was 1924.
It was a time when historians had begun to discern, with some clarity, what the twentieth century might become.

The First World War—having brought destruction and paralysis on a scale unknown to world history—had left all the great powers exhausted and enfeebled. Yet those same powers were testing the depths of endurance among their colonial peoples, imposing oppressive measures and intolerable laws upon them.

The colonial peoples who had survived the war, after seeing human beings perish like flies and ants, resolved that life was not so sweet that it must be preserved at the price of submission. They rose to confront the fanatics of imperialism.
The vast gulf between antiquity and modernity had greatly narrowed. The old and the new now stood face to face, clashing in every field.
Workers were casting away their servitude and drawing up lists of the rights they must obtain. Feudal landlordism and the zamindari system were breathing their last.

In southern India, the inaugural ceremony, as it were, of the movement for social justice and social revolution had begun. In the North, the foundations upon which communal conflicts would grow were being laid.

There was unity in the conviction that India must not remain enslaved by the British. But over the means by which freedom was to be won, deep disagreements and seemingly insoluble complications had arisen.
The people waited anxiously for someone—anyone—to say clearly what must happen. Such longing had become part of their daily life.

In 1924, the genius who would dispel a thousand years of darkness from Tamil Nadu and overturn every sphere of life in keeping with the demands of modernity—my teacher, Arignar Anna—was a fifteen-year-old student in high school, preparing himself to become the hero of a great history.
India was shaking off its long slumber and rising with a thrill.

The arrival of Gandhiji and his involvement in politics had created a new kind of awakening. Congress leaders were divided and bewildered by the question: “Complete independence—or self-government subject to imperial control?”

It was a time when Lajpat Rai, Subhas Chandra Bose, Sathyamurthi, Srinivasa Iyengar, C. R. Das, Motilal Nehru and others frequently differed with Gandhiji, engaging him in ceaseless debate and counter-debate.

The distinction of having Gandhiji formally assume the presidency of the Indian National Congress belongs to 1924 alone. Neither before nor afterwards did he serve as its official president. The organisational plans framed at the Belgaum session of 1924 were the very plans through which the Congress subsequently attained its full form.
It was a programme born in 1924 that transformed E. V. Ramasamy—whom the country today honours as Thanthai Periyar—into the Hero of Vaikom.
It was during this period that Thamizh Thendral Thiru. V. Kalyanasundara Mudaliar became a tempest against imperialism. It was in these days that the labour movement began to take root in Tamil Nadu.

Gandhi’s teachings of non-violence sank deep during this period. At the same time, cruel violence, such as that witnessed at Chauri Chaura, reared its head. Hindu–Muslim division, too, gained a foothold in northern India.
No other year of the twentieth century stood like so great a question mark as 1924.
Perhaps it is because I was born in that year that I, too, have remained a question mark to many throughout my life!
Gandhiji had been sentenced to six years in prison. But after serving only two, he was released in February 1924 by Viceroy Lord Reading because of failing health.

When he emerged from prison, he appeared an altogether renewed man—one who had mastered the strategies by which Indian politics might be guided with remarkable dexterity along a path between spiritualism and revolution.

During his two years in prison, it is said, Gandhiji memorised the Gita, read the Qur’an, and attempted to learn Tamil. Above all, he emerged with the clear conviction that the spark of India’s liberation could be found only in her villages.
It was also the year in which the young Jawaharlal Nehru, at the age of thirty-five and against his own wishes, was elected for the first time as General Secretary of the Indian National Congress.

How proudly, then, can I proclaim that I was born in 1924—when the words non-violence, truth, labour, imprisonment, freedom and the abolition of untouchability were being chanted like mantras from house to house and from person to person!
And that is not all.
The reforms of Kemal Pasha—reforms that I would repeatedly invoke with delight at every stage of my later political life—took shape and began to function in 1924.
It was in that year that the revolutionary hero Lenin died.
One epoch in world history came to an end, and another was born.

It was also the period when the Trotskyist doctrine of simultaneous bloody revolution throughout the world suffered defeat, and the communist movement descended from that abstraction to the principle that revolution must be shaped by the conditions peculiar to each country.
It was the moment when Stalin—whose fierce devotion to his leader Lenin could be compared with my own impassioned devotion to my leader Anna—rose to power.

Trotskyist theory, excessive intellectualism and a diplomacy that lacked confidence in itself were all struck down in that year. As though history wished to proclaim that the marks of firm leadership were the power of labour, unwavering loyalty to one’s leader and selfless zeal in serving the people, Stalin assumed command, in that very year, of a movement that held sway over one-third of the world.

In 1924, within the fortress prison of Bavaria, Adolf Hitler was writing his autobiography, Mein Kampf. The world knows how completely that autobiography would later overturn the ancient histories of so many nations and societies.

Nazism, fascism and communism were all rising vigorously in 1924. Yet in India, Gandhiji demonstrated another force: the singular respect that society could show an individual because of love, and the immense affection that a multitude could openly express.

One may say that this was also the period when another idea began to sprout: if Indian politics was to be conducted democratically, leaders possessing a distinctive stature within their own provinces would have to emerge—just as Gandhiji had emerged for India as a whole.
It was through this mingling of conflicting currents that the year 1924 made its way.
And it was in that year that I was born.

The year 1924, in which I was born, was also the year in which the Cauvery water agreement was concluded between Mysore and our Presidency. The agreement was made for a period of fifty years and was due to be reconsidered in 1974.
The people of Tamil Nadu were naturally anxious that any decisions made during that reconsideration should not injure the rights of their state.

Meanwhile, the Chief Minister of Mysore had ventured to proceed with the Hemavathi project despite the objections of the Tamil Nadu Government. The Government of Tamil Nadu had conveyed its emphatic opposition to the Union Government.

When I was born in 1924—the year of the Mysore–Tamil Nadu agreement—who could have imagined that I would one day become the Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu and stand against the Hemavathi dam project because it threatened to impair that agreement?
Even those who gave birth to me might affectionately have called me Raja—“king.” Would they ever have called me Manthiri—“minister”?
Before I write about myself, should I not offer at least a brief account of my parents?
Thirukkolili is one of the beautiful little villages of Thanjavur district. It is also called Thirukkuvalai. The village lies fifteen miles from Thiruvarur.
That is where our family lived.

There was a lovely temple tank, with green groves surrounding it. Opposite stood the temple of Siva. At the western boundary was the shrine of Muniyan; at the southern boundary, the temple of Ayyanar.
This was the Thirukkuvalai I knew.
Today, three-fourths of that beauty has disappeared.
But in my father’s time, it was a village of incomparable charm.

provenance.translator: Translation approved by the maintainer, July 2026
provenance.note: The maintainer's own rendering, adopted as the benchmark for the memoir translations. English paragraphing follows the argument's natural units rather than the OCR page-joins; the Tamil original remains authoritative.
